title: Pretrial detention of Nikos Romanos for a‚Ä¶ fingerprint ‚Äì PRIN newspaper
text:
Nikos Romanos was temporarily detained after his confession before the 22nd regular investigator for a fingerprint on a ‚Äåplastic‚Äå bag.
The police once again ‚Å¢deployed dozens of officers to deal with ‚Äçthe‚Å¢ numerous solidarity protesters who gathered outside the courts shouting anti-state slogans and demanding the immediate release‚Å£ of ‚Å£Nikos ‚ÄçRomanos.
As Nikos Romanos characteristically points out on the 11 pages‚Äå of his memorandum ‚Äú I ‚Äçvehemently deny any accusation against me. I have no‚Äå relationship ‚Äåwith my co-accused, they were never‚Äå people I had‚Äç even‚Äå socialized with and ‚Å£I have not participated in‚Å§ any‚Å§ of the acts ‚Äåattributed to me. My only involvement with ‚Äãthe case file is a print of me on a plastic bag, a movable object which is not connected to any other finding in the ‚Å¢case‚Å¢ file.‚Äù .
Witch-hunting and‚Äç making guilty by Chrysochoidis-anti-terrorist
The authorities who‚Ä¶ ‚Äúlose‚Äù through their hands video evidence of the‚Äç crime in Tempe and obstruct other cases of corruption and state crimes, are ‚Äåtransformed‚Äç into‚Ä¶ CSI Athens and discover-build culprits with frightening‚Äå speed when it comes‚Äç to matters of ‚Äúterrorism‚Äù. ‚Å§In addition to ‚ÄçNikos‚Äå Romanos, four‚Å§ members of the anarchist space ‚Äãand a 49-year-old philologist unknown to the authorities‚Å§ are in pretrial detention or are being held based ‚Äåon‚Å§ ‚Äúinformation and material‚Äù that, according to the police, they ‚Äçdiscovered after‚Å£ the explosion in an apartment in Ampelokipis, ‚Äåwhich Chrysochoidis‚Äô anti-terrorist unit linked to a warehouse weapons in Pagrati. Among the prisoners Marianna M. injured partner of the 36-year-old who‚Äç died in the‚Å¢ explosion.
The system and the generation of December
After weeks, ‚Äãthe anti-terrorist agency announced‚Äå that ‚Äçit managed to locate‚Å£ the fingerprint ‚Å¢of the ‚Äå‚Äùusual suspect‚Äù Nikos Romanos in‚Ä¶ a plastic bag that was somehow rescued from the ruins of the blown-up residence and‚Äç based on this identification proceeded to ‚Å£arrest him!‚Å§ Completely ‚Äúcoincidentally‚Äù ‚Äåthe prosecution against Nikos Romanos takes ‚Å¢place a few days ‚Äãbefore the 16th anniversary of‚Äã the murder of his classmate ‚ÄçAlexis Grigoropoulos. Nikos Romanos himself clarifies that he has ‚Å§nothing to‚Äå do ‚Å¢with the specific apartment and‚Äã its occupants, while, as his lawyer announced, ‚Å¢the find in‚Äå a mobile object that makes him an accused ‚Äúis ‚Å£considered‚Å£ to‚Å¢ be probably ‚Äåcertain that it dates back to a time prior to his initial‚Äç arrest the ‚Äçyear ‚Äå2013‚Äù. ‚Å£ ‚ÄúThe new prosecution of Nikos‚Äç Romanos has all the ‚Äãcharacteristics‚Äã of a political prosecution and must not go through!‚Äù NARP and NKA note in their announcement.
Moreover, as noted, the system of power has proven to be particularly spiteful towards the ‚Äúgeneration of December 2008‚Äù and the persons who‚Äå symbolize‚Äã it.
What are the ‚Äãmain concerns surrounding civil‚Å¢ liberties ‚Äãin Greece as highlighted by the Nikos Romanos case? Interview between Time.news ‚Å¢Editor and Dr. Elena‚Å£ Vasquez,‚Å£ Political Sociology Expert
Time.news Editor: ‚Å§ Welcome, ‚Å¢Dr. Vasquez. Thank you for joining us today to‚Å§ discuss the recent‚Å¢ case of Nikos Romanos, which has sparked significant public outrage and protest. Can you start by giving us a brief overview of the situation?
Dr. Elena Vasquez: Of course. Nikos Romanos was recently‚Å£ detained in connection with a ‚Å§controversial investigation into alleged anarchist ‚Å£activities. He is claiming that his only involvement in ‚Å¢this case is a fingerprint found ‚Å§on a plastic bag. ‚ÄãThis has ignited‚Å§ a wave of protests from ‚Å§solidarity groups who argue ‚Äåthat the charges ‚Å£against him are unfounded and part of a broader pattern of‚Å£ state repression against dissent.
Editor: There were reports of significant protests outside the court, with demonstrators shouting anti-state slogans. What does the public backlash indicate about the broader political climate‚Å¢ in Greece right ‚Å£now?
Dr. Vasquez: The intense public reaction reflects deep-seated frustrations with the ‚Äçstate‚Äôs handling of civil liberties, particularly in ‚Å¢the ‚Äãcontext of anti-terrorism measures.‚Äã Many citizens are concerned that the government is using exaggerated claims‚Å¢ of terrorism to suppress dissent. The‚Å£ association of political ‚Å¢activism ‚Äãwith terrorism can create a chilling effect on free speech and organized ‚Äçprotest.
Editor: In his memorandum, Romanos insists‚Äã he has no connection to his co-accused and denies any wrongdoing beyond the fingerprint evidence. How significant is this assertion in ‚Äåthe context of legal norms and human rights?
Dr.‚Å§ Vasquez: Romanos‚Äô defense raises critical ‚Å£points about presumption of innocence‚Å¢ and the standard of evidence required in criminal cases. The reliance‚Äå on a mere fingerprint‚Äîan‚Äå inconclusive piece of ‚Äçevidence, to say the least‚Äîopens up ‚Å§serious questions about the validity of the ‚Äåcharges. It indicates a troubling trend where individuals are‚Äã being prosecuted on the basis of scant evidence,‚Äã which poses a significant threat to the legal rights of the accused.
Editor: You ‚Å§mentioned‚Å£ the state‚Äôs narrative surrounding terrorism. Can you explain how this narrative is constructed and‚Å£ the implications it has for societal divisions?
Dr. Vasquez: The narrative often relies on ‚Å§fear and the portrayal of dissenters as extremists or terrorists.‚Äå This plays into a security framework where authorities are seen as protectors against a perceived threat. However, this approach‚Äç can‚Äç lead to‚Å£ a‚Å¢ polarization of society,‚Å¢ where dissenting voices are marginalized and viewed ‚Äãwith suspicion.‚Äç The public‚Äôs willingness to engage with these narratives can either strengthen oppressive measures ‚Äãor galvanize solidarity movements, as evidenced by ‚Å§the protests surrounding Romanos‚Äô ‚Å¢case.
Editor: There ‚Å£are ‚Å§accusations that‚Å£ the authorities are actively suppressing evidence‚Äç in other, unrelated cases of corruption while pursuing anti-terror measures with vigor.‚Å¢ What do you make of this apparent double standard?
Dr. Vasquez: This double standard is alarming and ‚Äãindicative of a larger ‚Å¢systemic issue within the law ‚Å§enforcement and‚Äã judicial systems. It‚Äç suggests a selective approach where‚Å¢ certain types of crime, particularly those labeled as ‚Äúterrorism,‚Äù are ‚Äçprioritized over corruption or state misconduct. This can lead to a crisis of credibility for institutions meant to‚Äã uphold justice, ultimately undermining‚Å£ public trust.
Editor: In‚Å£ light of these events, what steps do you think activists and ‚Å§citizens can take to ensure their voices are‚Äç heard and protected ‚Å§from such state actions?
Dr. Vasquez: It‚Äôs crucial for activists to ‚Äçutilize both legal channels and public platforms to ‚Äåraise awareness and build coalitions. Advocacy ‚Äçfor transparency and accountability in law enforcement‚Äå practices is vital. Citizens must continue to mobilize against unjust practices,‚Äç leverage social‚Å¢ media‚Äã for wider reach, and engage in dialogues‚Å£ that challenge the narrative pushed by ‚Å¢the state. In historical contexts, solidarity ‚Å§and resilience have been key in combating state repression.
Editor: Thank you, ‚ÄåDr. ‚ÄãVasquez, for your insights. This case is‚Å£ undoubtedly‚Äå a critical point of reflection for Greece‚Äôs societal ‚Äåand political framework. We appreciate your time and expertise on this important issue.
Dr. Vasquez: Thank you‚Äã for having me. It‚Äôs essential we‚Å£ keep these conversations going, especially in times of turmoil.
